era_context: Mid-14th century: The Hundred Years' War between England and France is in a phase of relative stalemate after the English victories at Crécy (1346) and Poitiers (1356). The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia under Orhan I. China is in the final decades of Yuan Dynasty rule, with Zhu Yuanzhang's forces rising in the south. The Timurid Empire is consolidating in Central Asia. Europe is recovering from the Black Death (1347–1353), experiencing labour shortages and social upheaval.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica and South America (limited documentary evidence)
Eastern Europe and Russia (sparse sources for this specific year)

events.title: Treaty of Brétigny Ends First Phase of Hundred Years' War
events.description: The Treaty of Brétigny (signed 8 May 1360) ended the first major phase of the Hundred Years' War between England and France. Under the treaty, England renounced its claims to the French throne in exchange for significant territorial concessions, including Aquitaine and other lands in southwestern France. The treaty also established a ransom for King John II of France, who had been captured at the Battle of Poitiers (1356). The treaty represented a major English victory, though it proved to be only a temporary peace; the war would resume in 1369.
key_figures: John II (King of France, held for ransom)
events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Brétigny is one of the most well-documented events of the 14th century. The date (8 May 1360) is secure in multiple sources.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang Consolidates Control in Southern China
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang continued his consolidation of power in southern China, eliminating rival warlords and establishing a stable administrative base. By 1360, his control over the Yangtze River valley was secure, and he was preparing for the final push against Yuan Dynasty rule in the north. This period of consolidation was critical to the eventual founding of the Ming Dynasty in 1368.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Zhu Yuanzhang's consolidation is well-established, but specific events within 1360 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Orhan I Consolidates Ottoman Power in Anatolia
events.description: Orhan I, the Ottoman Sultan, continued his consolidation of power in Anatolia. By 1360, the Ottoman Empire had become a significant regional power, controlling much of northwestern Anatolia. Orhan I's reign (1323–1362) was marked by steady territorial expansion, the establishment of Ottoman administrative structures, and the beginning of Ottoman expansion into the Balkans. His reign laid the foundation for the more aggressive expansion under his successor Murad I.
events.certainty_note: Orhan I's reign and expansion are well-documented in Ottoman historiography, but specific events in 1360 are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Leads to 1362-001 (Orhan I dies; Murad I succeeds)

events.title: Timur Consolidates Power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) continued his consolidation of power in Central Asia, defeating rival khans and establishing the foundation of the Timurid Empire. By 1360, Timur had secured control over much of Central Asia and was preparing for his campaigns into Persia and beyond. This period of consolidation was critical to Timur's eventual emergence as one of the most powerful military leaders of the medieval world.
key_figures: Timur (Tamerlane, r. 1369–1405, but consolidating power in 1360)
events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Timur's rise is well-documented, but specific events in 1360 are difficult to date precisely. Most detailed accounts were written decades or centuries later.

events.title: Peasant Unrest in England Following Labour Shortages
events.description: England experienced widespread peasant unrest and labour disputes in the aftermath of the Black Death. The severe labour shortage had given peasants unprecedented bargaining power, leading to demands for higher wages and better conditions. Local revolts and strikes occurred in various regions. These tensions would culminate in the Peasants' Revolt of 1381, but the underlying grievances were already acute in 1360.
key_figures: Various local peasant leaders (names largely unrecorded)

sources.name: English royal records and court documents

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of peasant unrest in the 1360s is well-documented, but specific events in 1360 are less precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Brétigny (1360-001) is sometimes presented as a decisive English victory, but modern scholarship has complicated this narrative. The treaty's terms were not fully implemented, and the renunciation of claims to the French throne was ambiguous and contested. The treaty proved to be only a temporary peace, and the underlying causes of the war remained unresolved. Some historians argue that the treaty represented a compromise rather than a clear English victory. Timur's consolidation of power (1360-004) is difficult to date precisely because later sources are retrospective and sometimes conflate or reorder events. The significance of peasant unrest (1360-005) is sometimes minimized in older historiography, which focused on elite political events.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1360 is uneven. The Treaty of Brétigny is exceptionally well-documented, being one of the most important diplomatic documents of the medieval period. European political and military events are reasonably well-attested in chronicles and royal records. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are comprehensive for major political developments. Ottoman sources are retrospective and less precise on dating. Central Asian sources (Timurid chronicles) are written decades or centuries after the events they describe. Social and economic history (peasant unrest) is underrepresented in surviving sources, which focus on elite political and military events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Southeast Asia have minimal documentary evidence for this year.
